Ecobank Group Annual Report 2025

40 YEARS OF BUILDING A
PAN-AFRICAN BANK
An ideal born into fragmentation and upheaval
In 1985, Africa had a fragmented financial landscape.
National boundaries and colonial legacies constrained vision and opportunity across our continent. Ecobank was founded to change that by integrating the continent's markets and supporting economic development.
20 years of private ownership:
connecting West and Central
Africa
Two decades saw Ecobank grow to 18 countries, proving that a home-grown institution was capable of uniting markets and mobilising capital across borders.
In 2006, Ecobank opened for public ownership through a historic triple listing on exchanges in Nigeria, Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire. Ecobank became the first panAfrican financial institution open for African shareholders.

A stronger Ecobank for a stronger Africa
Over four decades, Africa and Ecobank have changed dramatically. An ambition to build a pan-African financial institution has become a reality. Millions of people and businesses are connected across Africa and beyond.
As Africa's economies continue to grow and integrate,
Ecobank will continue to advance financial connectivity and access for everyone in Africa.
20 years of public ownership:
connecting our continent and beyond
Today, Ecobank connects more than 60% of the African continent through a single pan-African banking network. Four markets in Asia, Europe and the Middle East, expanding opportunities and investment.

The challenges were immense, yet the dream was larger still.
We aimed to demonstrate that
Africa could create institutions for itself, independently.
Gervais K. Djondo
Co-founder and former chairman of Ecobank

Ecobank is a leading financial institution connecting 34 developing and expanding countries across Africa. Approximately 13,889 employees serve the financial needs of over 30 million businesses and individuals (19 million primary banking relationships and 12 million customers acquired through partnerships), representing 61% of the continent. As of 31 December 2025, our total assets were $34.5 billion and total equity of $2.9bn. Our single-payment platform enables real-time payments across Africa, leveraging our geographic reach to serve customers. Our parent company, ETI, is publicly traded on three stock markets: the Nigerian
Exchange Group, the Bourse Régionale des Valeurs Mobilières (BRVM) in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire, and the Ghana Stock Exchange.

Ecobank Group Annual Report 2025
Dear Shareholders,
A LETTER FROM THE CHAIRMAN OF THE BOARD
Forty Years and Still Moving Forward
With Ambition
As Ecobank marks 40 years of serving customers, businesses and communities across Africa, this is a moment to look back with pride and ahead with purpose.
Over four decades, Ecobank has grown from a bold idea into a leading pan-African banking group, shaped by the belief that Africa deserves a bank built in Africa, for Africa, by Africans and today supported by a shareholder base and partners that span the globe. That combination of
African purpose and international confidence is both our identity and our competitive advantage.
In 2025, that sense of purpose remained clear with disciplined progress and continued focus on long-term value creation. Last year, I spoke of legacy, responsibility and the need to translate Ecobank's pan-African strengths into more sustainable value with stronger and more resilient governance. Those priorities remained central to the work of the Board throughout 2025 and continue to guide our stewardship of the institution.
Throughout 2025, the Group anniversary celebration brought together Ecobankers, partners, customers, governments, regulators and shareholders from across
Africa and beyond. We are deeply grateful to all who joined us in marking this occasion. Above all, we wish to recognise the staff across every corner of the continent whose dedication and professionalism make Ecobank what it is every single day. This anniversary belongs to every person who has built, served and sustained this institution at any time during the last four decades.
Chairman of the ETI Board with some of Ecobank's Founding Fathers during the Gala Dinner celebrating the 40th anniversary.
Lomé - December 4, 2025.
Our role as a Board is to provide oversight, to challenge management constructively, to ensure that risks are well understood, and to protect the interests of shareholders and the wider institution. It is equally to ensure that the Bank remains resilient, well-governed and strategically positioned to create sustainable long-term value. This remained an important responsibility in 2025.
A Demanding but Rewarding Year for the Continent
The external environment in 2025 remained complex. African economies continued to face currency pressures, elevated financing costs and, in some markets, uneven growth.
Geopolitical uncertainties and the residual effects of prior years' fiscal stress in certain countries created ongoing headwinds for businesses and households across our footprint. Against this backdrop, the Group's performance was not only strong in absolute terms but particularly meaningful in context, reflecting the resilience of our diversified model, the depth of the pan-African franchise, and the quality of execution across the Group. Our pan-African model was built to not only remain operational and relevant but also to succeed precisely when conditions become difficult, and 2025 tested that premise, meaningfully.

Strong Performance, Sustained Momentum
In 2025, Ecobank delivered strong growth in key earnings measures. Net revenue increased by 17% to $2.45 billion and profit before tax rose by 21% to $801 million. Profit attributable to ETI shareholders increased by 22% to $407 million. Return on tangible equity was 27.8%, while the cost-to-income ratio improved on last year's record-setting pace to 48.3% in 2025. Earnings per share increased by 23% to 1.68 US cents ($0.017), while tangible book value per share rose by 82% to 7.65 US cents ($0.076).
For the Board, the main point is what these results say about the robustness of the Group and the progress being made in strengthening the institution. It is notable that 2025 is the third consecutive year in which Ecobank has delivered revenues above $2 billion, a threshold that was once aspirational and now reflects the durability of the Group's earnings platform. Beyond the headline numbers, the
Board's focus remains on the sustainability and quality of earnings, capital resilience and the consistency of value creation over time.
The market has taken notice: In 2025, ETI's share price soared by 49.6% in NGN terms (60.2% in dollar terms). And, over the last 24 months leading up to my letter in mid-May
2026, ETI's share price has impressively surged by 305.0% in
NGN terms (323.9% in US dollar terms). This remarkable growth is a powerful testament to investors' confidence in the Group's fundamentals and bright future.

The Group's standing was further recognised externally when it was named Best Bank in Africa by Global Finance, among other significant recognitions during the year. The
Board is also pleased to acknowledge the contribution of our top-performing affiliates. In terms of both revenue and profit before tax, Ecobank Ghana, Ecobank Côte d'Ivoire and Ecobank Guinea were the strongest contributors to
Group performance in 2025 reflecting disciplined management and the depth of our franchise in these markets. We extend our appreciation to the leadership and staff of each of these subsidiaries for their outstanding contribution to the Group's results.
International capital markets have continued to respond positively throughout the year, signalling sustained investor confidence in Ecobank. In May 2025, ETI returned to the market with a tap issuance to raise an additional $125 million at lower market prices on the existing $400 million Eurobond it issued in 2024, which was oversubscribed by investors from
Africa, Europe, the UK, and beyond.
Visit by the Chairman of the ETI Board to Ecobank Guinea Head
Office: The Chairman of the Ecobank Guinea Board, M. Mamady
Youla, along with the Managing Director of Ecobank Guinea, M.
Diawadou Bah, and members of the Ecobank Guinea staff, participated in the visit - November 2025.
Rewarding Patience: Restoring the Dividend
For the first time since 2023, the Board is recommending a dividend. Last year, I explained why the Board deemed that capital discipline had to come first and asked shareholders to trust that patience would be rewarded. I am pleased to report that it has. The Board has approved the recommendation of a dividend of $40 million, equivalent to
0.16 US cents per share ($0.0016), for shareholder approval at the Annual General Meeting. This reflects the Board's confidence in the Group's improving capital position and earnings trajectory, and builds on the progress made since the last dividend payment of $28 million for the 2022 financial year.

Our capital allocation approach remains clear: to preserve balance sheet strength, support priority investments and capital needs across the Group, and restore sustainable shareholder returns over time. Many of our more than
640,000 shareholders hold smaller positions and depend on dividends as an important part of their return, and the Board takes that responsibility seriously.
We are therefore committed to rebuilding a sustainable and disciplined pattern of distributions that serves all shareholders, large and small.

A Stronger Balance Sheet, a More Resilient Institution
Capital and balance sheet resilience continued to receive close Board attention throughout the year. As at year-end, the Group's estimated CET1 ratio stood at 13.2% and total capital adequacy ratio at 16.8%, above regulatory minimum requirements. Customer deposits increased by 24% to $25.3 billion, further strengthening the Group's liquidity and funding profile. At the same time, the Board has continued to approach capital allocation with discipline and a long-term perspective, recognising that stronger performance does not by itself settle questions around capital flexibility, upstream capacity or distributable value. These matters still require prudence and careful judgement.
Turnaround and Transformation Markets
Nigeria remained an important area of Board focus during the year, as it has been for some time. The Board is encouraged by the progress made in strengthening the business and addressing legacy matters, in order to better seize the opportunity of Nigeria's long-term potential as
Africa's largest economy. Our approach continues to be disciplined and patient: we are focused on building a strong, well-governed franchise capable of generating consistent returns over time. This means ensuring the foundations are solid enough to enable acceleration, while we engage constructively with regulators, and continue to support local management as it navigates a complex but opportunityrich environment. Nigeria is a market where Ecobank is proud to have developed deep roots.
We also kept close focus on subscale and transformation markets, where performance is improving but sustained discipline is still needed to build stronger and more consistently profitable businesses over time. Particularly encouraging progress was observed in certain turnaround markets within the CESA region, where profitability and efficiency metrics improved materially during the year.
During 2025, the Group completed the sale of its
Mozambique subsidiary to FDH Bank Plc, a Malawian financial institution, marking our full exit from that market after more than a decade of presence. This was a considered decision, consistent with our strategy of focusing resources on markets where we can compete effectively and generate sustainable returns. The Group worked closely with local regulators and the buyer to ensure continuity of service, the protection of employee contracts and conditions, and the preservation of the bank's role as a meaningful contributor to the local economy. The institution continues to serve its customers and communities under new ownership, proof that the transition has been managed responsibly and in the interests of all stakeholders.

Governance, Accountability and Shareholder
Matters
Governance remained a priority in 2025. The Board continued to strengthen its oversight of risk governance, capital allocation, succession planning, executive accountability, remuneration alignment with performance, and regulatory engagement. Governance is not simply a matter of compliance for the Board; it is a strategic enabler of performance, confidence and institutional resilience.
The year also served as a reminder that strong institutions are tested as much in moments of scrutiny as in periods of growth. During 2025, the Board had to manage a challenging governance matter. Independent processes were activated promptly, proper procedures were followed in full, and the matter was handled in accordance with the highest standards of governance. The Board is satisfied that its response reflected the maturity that strong institutions must demonstrate when tested.
What the episode also demonstrated, and what the Board wishes to recognise unreservedly, was the conduct of
Ecobank's staff throughout. Despite the uncertainty that such situations inevitably create, Ecobankers across the
Group did not lose focus. They continued to serve our customers, execute on strategy and deliver results, meeting and in many cases exceeding the ambitious targets set by the Board through management. That combination of professional integrity and commercial focus, maintained under pressure, speaks to the character of this institution and its people.
The Board also gave close attention to Ecobank's sustainability agenda during the year. As a pan-African institution operating across 34 African countries, Ecobank has both the opportunity and the responsibility to contribute meaningfully to the continent's sustainable development -  through responsible financing, financial inclusion, sound environmental practices and positive community impact.
Shareholder matters required focused attention throughout the year. Nedbank publicly announced the divestment of its 21.22% shareholding in ETI to Bosquet Investments
Limited.
The necessary regulatory processes and approvals from the relevant authorities have now been completed. We welcome Bosquet Investments Limited to the Ecobank shareholder base and look forward to constructive engagement with them, while continuing to safeguard the interests of the institution and all of its shareholders.
The Power of Pan-African Scale
Ecobank's geographic scale remains one of the Group's clearest strengths. The Board continues to see real value in the way our network brings together customers, markets and capital across Africa through payments, trade, remittances and broader client activity. In 2025, payment revenues increased by 14% to $305 million, underlining the growing strategic relevance of this platform to the Group's long-term growth ambitions. The Group also made meaningful headway in fintech partnerships and in crossborder remittances  -  areas where our pan-African footprint gives us a genuine and distinctive advantage. The Group
Chief Executive Officer's letter sets out these developments in greater detail. The task now is to make sure that this scale delivers even more clearly in the form of stronger execution, deeper client relevance and more consistent value for shareholders. How do we turn this scale and reach into stronger and more sustainable value for shareholders? In our view, that means staying disciplined in how capital is allocated, maintaining strong governance, holding leadership to clear account and delivering returns more consistently over time. That is the Board's agenda, and it remains unchanged.
Firmly in Execution, Confidently Moving Forward
The turn that began a few years ago is now firmly established. We are not in transition, we are in execution.
The institution is better governed, better capitalised and better positioned than it has been in many years. There is still work to do, particularly in markets where performance has not yet matched potential. But the direction is clear and the foundation is strong. We move forward with confidence in what Ecobank can deliver for its shareholders, its customers and the continent it was built to serve.
I thank my fellow directors for their commitment during an exacting year that made significant demands on the Board.
I wish once more to recognise the professionalism and dedication of Ecobankers across the Group, whose commitment to serving our customers and supporting one another remained evident throughout the year. I also thank our shareholders, regulators and customers for their continued trust and support.
Our agenda remains clear: to provide steady stewardship, maintain discipline in capital allocation, and support management through constructive challenge and sound judgement, while helping to ensure that Ecobank continues to grow stronger and create greater long-term value for shareholders.
Yours sincerely,
Papa Madiaw Ndiaye
Chairman of the Board

A Strong Year, Built on Disciplined Execution
Beyond the headline numbers, we are proud that the quality of our growth improved. We grew customer deposits, improved our funding mix, expanded lending selectively, increased transaction volumes and value across payment channels, and remained disciplined in our spending. That combination supported stronger returns and a more balanced business that is well positioned for the future.
Rewarding Shareholder Patience
Our stronger capital position also supports a proposal to resume shareholder distributions. We were pleased to propose a dividend of $40 million, equivalent to 0.16 US cents per share, subject to shareholders' approval at the
Annual General Meeting. This compares with the last dividend of $28 million, or 0.11 US cents per share, paid on the basis of our 2022 results. Under the Returns pillar of our strategy, this proposed payout reflects both stronger underlying performance and our commitment to enhancing shareholder returns in a disciplined and sustainable way.
This is an important step. Ecobank has not paid a dividend since 2022. Any decision to resume distributions must therefore rest on financial discipline, capital resilience and a clear view of the investment still needed to support future growth. As such, we are pleased that ETI's share price increased by 50% in NGN terms to N41.90 or 60% in US dollar terms to $0.029 during the year, contributing further to total shareholder returns.
We recognise the patience of our shareholders, including those who have stood with Ecobank since its listing in
September 2006. Their continued trust has mattered, particularly through periods when capital preservation and reinvestment had to take priority over distributions. The proposed dividend is therefore an important step in restoring a more consistent path of shareholder returns, while maintaining the discipline needed to support future growth.
Our approach remains balanced: reward shareholders appropriately, while preserving the strength and flexibility we need to keep executing our strategy.
Operating Across a Mixed Environment
Our 2025 performance came through in a mixed operating environment. In UEMOA, growth remained strong and inflation eased. In Anglophone West Africa (AWA), including
Ghana, conditions improved gradually as fiscal discipline strengthened and inflation moderated. Across Central,
Eastern and Southern Africa (CESA), several markets delivered good momentum despite external and domestic pressures. The common thread across our regions is that
Africa continues to grow, and the case for building resilient intra-African financial infrastructure remains strong.
Forty Years of Connecting Africa
Ecobank was founded in 1985 on a clear idea: Africa needed a bank built to connect markets, businesses and ambition across borders. That idea remains highly relevant today.
As we mark 40 years of Ecobank, we do so with pride in what has been built and with confidence in what lies ahead.
From our first subsidiary in Togo in 1988, we have grown into a pan-African banking group serving over 30 million customers across account-based and partnership channels, with total assets of $34.5 billion, customer deposits of $25.3 billion and gross loans of $12.8 billion.
Today, our footprint spans 34 sub-Saharan African countries and is complemented by operations in France, the United
Kingdom, United Arab Emirates and China, helping clients connect African opportunities to global trade and capital flows. Our network is one of our greatest strengths, not just because of its scale, but because of what it allows customers and clients to do: receive, save and move money, make payments, manage liquidity, finance trade and grow across borders.

Stronger Earnings, Better Quality Growth
What matters to us is not just growth, but the quality and durability of that growth. Our revenue base is broader and more balanced than before. In 2025, 42.4% of Group revenue came from non-interest revenue, while net fee and commission income accounted for 24.4% of net revenue. Net interest income increased to $1.4 billion, while non-interest revenue rose to $1.0 billion. Fee and commission income increased to $676 million. That tells us performance is being supported not only by lending, but also by payments, transaction services, treasury solutions and other fee-based activities across the franchise.

Ecobank Group Annual Report 2025
Dear Shareholders,
A LETTER FROM THE CHAIRMAN OF THE BOARD
Forty Years and Still Moving Forward
With Ambition
As Ecobank marks 40 years of serving customers, businesses and communities across Africa, this is a moment to look back with pride and ahead with purpose.
Over four decades, Ecobank has grown from a bold idea into a leading pan-African banking group, shaped by the belief that Africa deserves a bank built in Africa, for Africa, by Africans and today supported by a shareholder base and partners that span the globe. That combination of
African purpose and international confidence is both our identity and our competitive advantage.
In 2025, that sense of purpose remained clear with disciplined progress and continued focus on long-term value creation. Last year, I spoke of legacy, responsibility and the need to translate Ecobank's pan-African strengths into more sustainable value with stronger and more resilient governance. Those priorities remained central to the work of the Board throughout 2025 and continue to guide our stewardship of the institution.
Throughout 2025, the Group anniversary celebration brought together Ecobankers, partners, customers, governments, regulators and shareholders from across
Africa and beyond. We are deeply grateful to all who joined us in marking this occasion. Above all, we wish to recognise the staff across every corner of the continent whose dedication and professionalism make Ecobank what it is every single day. This anniversary belongs to every person who has built, served and sustained this institution at any time during the last four decades.
Chairman of the ETI Board with some of Ecobank's Founding Fathers during the Gala Dinner celebrating the 40th anniversary.
Lomé - December 4, 2025.
Our role as a Board is to provide oversight, to challenge management constructively, to ensure that risks are well understood, and to protect the interests of shareholders and the wider institution. It is equally to ensure that the Bank remains resilient, well-governed and strategically positioned to create sustainable long-term value. This remained an important responsibility in 2025.
A Demanding but Rewarding Year for the Continent
The external environment in 2025 remained complex. African economies continued to face currency pressures, elevated financing costs and, in some markets, uneven growth.
Geopolitical uncertainties and the residual effects of prior years' fiscal stress in certain countries created ongoing headwinds for businesses and households across our footprint. Against this backdrop, the Group's performance was not only strong in absolute terms but particularly meaningful in context, reflecting the resilience of our diversified model, the depth of the pan-African franchise, and the quality of execution across the Group. Our pan-African model was built to not only remain operational and relevant but also to succeed precisely when conditions become difficult, and 2025 tested that premise, meaningfully.

Strong Performance, Sustained Momentum
In 2025, Ecobank delivered strong growth in key earnings measures. Net revenue increased by 17% to $2.45 billion and profit before tax rose by 21% to $801 million. Profit attributable to ETI shareholders increased by 22% to $407 million. Return on tangible equity was 27.8%, while the cost-to-income ratio improved on last year's record-setting pace to 48.3% in 2025. Earnings per share increased by 23% to 1.68 US cents ($0.017), while tangible book value per share rose by 82% to 7.65 US cents ($0.076).
For the Board, the main point is what these results say about the robustness of the Group and the progress being made in strengthening the institution. It is notable that 2025 is the third consecutive year in which Ecobank has delivered revenues above $2 billion, a threshold that was once aspirational and now reflects the durability of the Group's earnings platform. Beyond the headline numbers, the
Board's focus remains on the sustainability and quality of earnings, capital resilience and the consistency of value creation over time.
The market has taken notice: In 2025, ETI's share price soared by 49.6% in NGN terms (60.2% in dollar terms). And, over the last 24 months leading up to my letter in mid-May
2026, ETI's share price has impressively surged by 305.0% in
NGN terms (323.9% in US dollar terms). This remarkable growth is a powerful testament to investors' confidence in the Group's fundamentals and bright future.

The Group's standing was further recognised externally when it was named Best Bank in Africa by Global Finance, among other significant recognitions during the year. The
Board is also pleased to acknowledge the contribution of our top-performing affiliates. In terms of both revenue and profit before tax, Ecobank Ghana, Ecobank Côte d'Ivoire and Ecobank Guinea were the strongest contributors to
Group performance in 2025 reflecting disciplined management and the depth of our franchise in these markets. We extend our appreciation to the leadership and staff of each of these subsidiaries for their outstanding contribution to the Group's results.
International capital markets have continued to respond positively throughout the year, signalling sustained investor confidence in Ecobank. In May 2025, ETI returned to the market with a tap issuance to raise an additional $125 million at lower market prices on the existing $400 million Eurobond it issued in 2024, which was oversubscribed by investors from
Africa, Europe, the UK, and beyond.

Rewarding Patience: Restoring the Dividend
For the first time since 2023, the Board is recommending a dividend. Last year, I explained why the Board deemed that capital discipline had to come first and asked shareholders to trust that patience would be rewarded. I am pleased to report that it has. The Board has approved the recommendation of a dividend of $40 million, equivalent to
0.16 US cents per share ($0.0016), for shareholder approval at the Annual General Meeting. This reflects the Board's confidence in the Group's improving capital position and earnings trajectory, and builds on the progress made since the last dividend payment of $28 million for the 2022 financial year.

A Stronger Balance Sheet, a More Resilient Institution
Capital and balance sheet resilience continued to receive close Board attention throughout the year. As at year-end, the Group's estimated CET1 ratio stood at 13.2% and total capital adequacy ratio at 16.8%, above regulatory minimum requirements. Customer deposits increased by 24% to $25.3 billion, further strengthening the Group's liquidity and funding profile. At the same time, the Board has continued to approach capital allocation with discipline and a long-term perspective, recognising that stronger performance does not by itself settle questions around capital flexibility, upstream capacity or distributable value. These matters still require prudence and careful judgement.
Turnaround and Transformation Markets
Nigeria remained an important area of Board focus during the year, as it has been for some time. The Board is encouraged by the progress made in strengthening the business and addressing legacy matters, in order to better seize the opportunity of Nigeria's long-term potential as
Africa's largest economy. Our approach continues to be disciplined and patient: we are focused on building a strong, well-governed franchise capable of generating consistent returns over time. This means ensuring the foundations are solid enough to enable acceleration, while we engage constructively with regulators, and continue to support local management as it navigates a complex but opportunityrich environment. Nigeria is a market where Ecobank is proud to have developed deep roots.
We also kept close focus on subscale and transformation markets, where performance is improving but sustained discipline is still needed to build stronger and more consistently profitable businesses over time. Particularly encouraging progress was observed in certain turnaround markets within the CESA region, where profitability and efficiency metrics improved materially during the year.
During 2025, the Group completed the sale of its
Mozambique subsidiary to FDH Bank Plc, a Malawian financial institution, marking our full exit from that market after more than a decade of presence. This was a considered decision, consistent with our strategy of focusing resources on markets where we can compete effectively and generate sustainable returns. The Group worked closely with local regulators and the buyer to ensure continuity of service, the protection of employee contracts and conditions, and the preservation of the bank's role as a meaningful contributor to the local economy. The institution continues to serve its customers and communities under new ownership, proof that the transition has been managed responsibly and in the interests of all stakeholders.

Governance remained a priority in 2025. The Board continued to strengthen its oversight of risk governance, capital allocation, succession planning, executive accountability, remuneration alignment with performance, and regulatory engagement. Governance is not simply a matter of compliance for the Board; it is a strategic enabler of performance, confidence and institutional resilience.
The year also served as a reminder that strong institutions are tested as much in moments of scrutiny as in periods of growth. During 2025, the Board had to manage a challenging governance matter. Independent processes were activated promptly, proper procedures were followed in full, and the matter was handled in accordance with the highest standards of governance. The Board is satisfied that its response reflected the maturity that strong institutions must demonstrate when tested.

Group did not lose focus. They continued to serve our customers, execute on strategy and deliver results, meeting and in many cases exceeding the ambitious targets set by the Board through management. As a pan-African institution operating across 34 African countries, Ecobank has both the opportunity and the responsibility to contribute meaningfully to the continent's sustainable development -  through responsible financing, financial inclusion, sound environmental practices and positive community impact.

Limited.
The necessary regulatory processes and approvals from the relevant authorities have now been completed. 400 basis points above regulatory minima on a consolidated basis.
What matters is not only where those ratios ended the year, but how we managed towards them. We have become more disciplined in how we allocate capital, how we assess returns and where we choose to compete. We use return metrics more actively in our scorecards. We direct resources more deliberately to the businesses and markets where we see the strongest potential. And we are prepared to make difficult decisions where returns do not justify continued capital commitment.
A clear example of our commitment to capital discipline was our decision to divest the Mozambique business because it did not meet our performance and return criteria in a competitive market. For us, capital discipline is not a side topic; it is central to ensuring that growth translates into shareholder value.
Throughout the year, we remained focused on prudent liquidity management, funding diversification and longertenor funding. We also continued to stress test the balance sheet, identify pressure points early, act where required, and even enhanced our central provisions to give us cover for any future challenges. Capital strength did not happen by itself. It came from stronger earnings, tighter balance-sheet management and very deliberate choices across the Group.
Execution Across the Franchise
Both of our core business lines made a strong contribution.
In Corporate and Investment Banking (CIB), revenue increased by 21% to $1.3 billion and profit before tax rose by 40% to $698 million. This performance was broad-based.
It reflected stronger treasury performance, higher client activity in foreign exchange and fixed income, growth in cash management fees, increased payment volumes, and continued momentum in trade finance and asset management. In Global Transaction Banking, we won more than 75 major cash-management mandates from multinationals, development finance institutions, humanitarian agencies and regional corporates. Our Trade
Finance business also supported $610 million in commodity financing, reinforcing our ambition to be Africa's leading trade partner.
In Consumer and Commercial Banking (CCB), revenue increased by 15% to $1.2 billion and profit before tax rose by
25% to $480 million. Here too, the result came from deliberate execution. We focused on expanding access for both new and existing customers, improving and broadening our product and customer solutions, deepening customer engagement, increasing lending activity, and growing card, deposit-related and payment fees through higher transaction volumes. We broadened digital account opening in more markets, installed over 500 new ATMs, extended our Direct Sales Agent network into 22 markets and added over 1,000 new personnel. In Commercial
Banking, we strengthened relationships with SMEs, particularly in agribusiness, and continued to build more specialised segment capabilities.
What this shows is that our model works best when we combine scale, reach and execution discipline across the network. It also shows the value of banking the

ecosystem end-to-end, serving corporates, public sector clients, SMEs and consumers through one connected pan-African franchise.
Our regional performance also showed the value of diversification. UEMOA, AWA and CESA all delivered strong revenue growth and healthy returns on equity. UEMOA remained strong, with profit before tax rising 11% (+6% in constant currency) and return on equity of 26.8%. AWA delivered profit before tax growth of 28% (+18% in constant currency), with good momentum across both business lines.
CESA, our fastest growing and highest performing region, recorded profit before tax growth of 52% (+50% in constant currency) and return on equity of 36.1%. Our Nigeria affiliate grew its revenues by 22% (+28% in constant currency) and pre-provision profit by 96% (+113% in constant currency), however, this was offset by necessary impairment provisions which impacted its profits and returns. Our broad-based performance in other regions helped offset weaker performance in Nigeria and again showed the strength of our regional footprint.
You can find more information about the performance and strategic plans for each of our business lines in the letters from our Group Executives that follow my letter. I encourage you to do so.

Nigeria: Facing the Issues,
Building Better Quality Growth
While the Group delivered strong overall results, we also took decisive action in areas where performance needed to improve, most notably in Nigeria.
Nigeria remains one of Ecobank's largest market opportunities, and transforming the business there remains an important part of our GTR strategy. At the same time, it remains a business that needs active, focused management attention. In 2025, it was one of our biggest priorities.
Ecobank Nigeria has met the Central Bank of Nigeria's minimum capital requirement of N200 billion for national banks. This requirement was met in December 2024. As communicated previously, Ecobank Nigeria's capital adequacy ratio is below the regulatory minimum of 10%. This is a matter that the Board and Management of Ecobank
Nigeria are actively addressing through a combination of measures, while providing regular updates to the Central
Bank of Nigeria.
In the fourth quarter of 2025, as part of our measures to exit the Central Bank of Nigeria's regulatory forbearance, we took significant impairments in Nigeria, particularly within the CIB portfolio. This resulted in a pre-tax loss of $31 million for the year and a non-performing loan ratio of 42.1%. These actions made the turnaround more demanding in the near term, but they were necessary. They brought greater transparency to legacy issues and allowed us to address the difficult matters directly rather than carry them forward.
What matters now is execution. We are working closely with the regulator. We are actively managing capital and asset-quality issues and we are reshaping the business towards areas where we see better-quality growth. That shift is already visible. In 2025, CCB in Nigeria grew by 23% year on year, driven by deeper client engagement and growing transaction volumes. We still have work to do, but the path is clear: deal with the legacy issues, restore compliance, and build a better-quality business underneath.
Growth, Transformation and Returns in Action
Our Growth, Transformation and Returns strategy remains the right framework for the next phase of Ecobank's development.
Growth means deepening customer relationships in the businesses and markets where we can win sustainably.
Transformation means having the courage to reinvent the way we work and the solutions we provide our clients, while leveraging evolving technology and artificial intelligence solutions to serve our clients better. In doing so, we have sharpened our clarity of focus in each market so that we can compete more effectively. We have also upgraded our risk frameworks and appetite in line with changing

geopolitical and macroeconomic fundamentals. These actions enhance the way we run the Group.
Our Returns pillar focuses on having a disciplined approach to capital management and allocation, converting our scale, network and capabilities into stronger and more sustainable shareholder value. In particular, we have focused on and made significant progress on dividend upstreaming by subsidiaries, which has grown from $217 million in 2024 to $303 million in 2025. This has strengthened our central liquidity and capital position, supporting our proposed dividend payment.
In 2025, we made progress across all three areas. We improved our payments, cash management and trade finance capabilities. We continued to invest in technology and digital platforms to support faster product delivery, better integration across markets and a stronger customer experience. We also took steps to improve how the Group works internally, simplifying processes, strengthening governance and making execution more disciplined. Across the Group, we remained focused on doing the simple things well, with consistency, accountability and better followthrough. We are not getting distracted; we are taking the right difficult decisions and executing with agility and discipline.
Purpose Beyond Banking
Our purpose also extends beyond banking. Through the
Ecobank Foundation, we continued to support social impact across Africa, with programmes focused on health, education and gender equality. In 2025, the Foundation strengthened its role as a platform for partnerships, including work on health and nutrition, inclusive digital education, and women's financial empowerment. This reflects a simple belief: stronger communities help build stronger economies.
In education, the Foundation concluded its three-year "Transforming Africa Through Education" campaign, exceeding its initial targets by establishing 66 IT laboratories and training 46,414 children and young people across the continent. Through the enhanced Ellevate programme, launched in nine African countries in March 2025, Ecobank also continued to support women entrepreneurs with financing, mentoring and advisory services. These initiatives are part of how the Group connects financial inclusion with broader economic inclusion.
Focused on the Next Phase
As we look ahead to 2026, our focus remains on disciplined execution. Subject to market conditions, we expect net revenue growth in the low-to-mid single digits, with operating expense growth in the low single digits. We will continue to manage the business towards a cost-to-income ratio of 48% to 50%, while reducing cost of risk and improving asset quality over time.
How we deliver that is just as important as the targets themselves. In CIB, we see further room to deepen wallet share, grow transaction banking, expand trade finance and build sector expertise in the industries that matter most across our markets. In CCB, we see opportunities to grow customer numbers, deepen primary banking relationships, increase transactional activity and scale lending carefully within a stronger risk framework. In payments, we continue to see significant opportunity across domestic payments, cross-border flows, remittances and Banking-as-a-Service, supported by investment in technology, partnerships and our central platform.
These targets are linked to clear delivery levers across the business: stronger customer acquisition, deeper client relationships, better transaction flows, continued digital investment, tighter cost discipline and more active risk management. That is how we intend to strengthen returns, improve earnings quality and continue building a bank that is more efficient, more connected and better positioned for the future.
Building the Ecobank of the Future
As we mark 40 years of Ecobank, I do not see an institution that has reached its destination. I see one becoming more focused on what it was built to do. Africa still needs connection, but now at a new level: faster, more digital, more secure, more integrated and more inclusive.
That is the work in front of us.
To our employees, thank you for what you deliver every day.
To our regulators, thank you for your partnership and your challenge to keep improving. To our customers, thank you for trusting us in the moments that matter. And to our shareholders, thank you for your trust, patience, scrutiny and belief.
Our task now is clear, even if demanding to deliver: to execute consistently across 34 African markets so that customers choose Ecobank first, every time.
Yours sincerely,
Jeremy Awori
Group Chief Executive Officer

Business review
Since the launch of the Growth, Transformation and Returns strategy in November 2023, the Company has continued to drive sustainable business and financial performance for the long-term value for all stakeholders, primarily across its four geographical regions, namely, Anglophone West Africa (AWA), Central, Eastern and Southern Africa (CESA),
Francophone West Africa (UEMOA) and Nigeria, and its two business segments, Corporate and Investment Banking and
Consumer and Commercial Banking, leveraging on transformation initiatives. A comprehensive review of the
Group's businesses in 2025 can be found in the Annual Report's
Business and Financial Review section on pages 116 to 135.
Highlight of results
ETI, as a holding company reported a profit after tax of $114 million for the financial year that ended on 31 December
2025. Detailed results for this period can be found in the
Parent Company's financial statements on pages 266 to 269 of this Annual Report. The Board of Directors approved the
Parent Company's financial statements and the Group's consolidated financial statements for the year ended 31
December 2025 at a meeting held on 12 March 2026. Messrs.
Papa Madiaw Ndiaye, Jeremy Awori and Ayo Adepoju were authorised to sign the accounts on behalf of the Board. Both
ETI's and the Ecobank Group's accounts are prepared in accordance with International Financial Reporting
Standards (IFRS).
Share capital
The Authorised Capital of ETI is $1,250,000,000 as of
31 December 2025.
Trading in ETI shares: Approximately 845,948,601 units of ETI ordinary shares were traded on the NGX, Lagos, the BRVM,
Abidjan, and the GSE, Accra, during 2025.
Major changes in shareholding
In accordance with its strategic decision to focus on their core markets in Southern and Eastern Africa, Nedbank Group divested its shareholding from the Company. Bosquet
Investment Limited, an Africa focused investment company, has now acquired Nedbank's 21.22% stake in ETI. The Board welcomes Bosquet Investments Limited as a significant shareholder of ETI.

Ecobank Group Annual Report 2025
Results commentary: CESA, our best performing region, reported a profit before tax of $450 million, an increase of $154 million, or 52% (50% in constant currency). ROE was 36.1%.
Net revenue increased by $188 million, or 28% (29% in constant currency), to $849 million. Net interest income increased by $82 million, or 22%, to $462 million, driven by growth in lending across business lines and higher trade loans in the commercial sector. Higher funding costs partially offset this growth. Non-interest revenues increased by $106 million, or 38%, to $387 million, primarily due to increased fees from client-driven foreign currency sales in Commercial banking, higher card and deposit-related fees from an increase in transaction volumes in the Consumer business.
Operating expenses increased by $38 million, or 11% (11% in constant currency), to $375 million, driven mainly by staffrelated compensation accruals and tax expenses. The cost-to-income ratio improved to 44.2% in 2025, down from
51.0% in 2024, reflecting positive operating leverage.
Impairment charges on financial assets rose by $4 million, or 14%, to $24 million. The driving factors were a $20 million increase in gross impairment charges on loans due to loan growth, and a $3 million decrease in impairment charges on other assets (excluding loans). This increase in overall impairment charges was partially offset by a $17 million rise in loan recoveries.
Full-year 2026 guidance
Management anticipates that gross loans and deposits will grow at a low-to-mid single-digit rate. The growth in loans is expected to be driven by trade loans, digitally enabled loans across both CCB, and loans in our CIB segments.
Management projects a decrease in the non-performing loan ratio to 6%-8% by 2026, indicating steady improvements in portfolio quality and credit discipline. Additionally, it is expected that the coverage ratio will remain above 80%.
Net revenue growth is forecast in the low to mid-single digits, while operating expenses are expected to rise in the low single digits. The cost-to-income ratio should remain between 48% and 50% to support planned technology investments, business expenses, and facilitate further revenue growth.
Furthermore, management expects the cost of risk to range between 250 and 350 basis points, taking into account the current impairment trajectory and potential volatility resulting from the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
Outlook
Looking into 2026, the outlook for Africa is cautiously optimistic. Inflation is retreating, growth is holding up, and fiscal discipline is improving across most countries.
However, sustaining this progress will depend on continued structural reforms, debt management, and the ability to withstand external shocks from global trade disruptions and commodity price volatility. And more importantly, the potential adverse impact of the ongoing tensions in the Middle East. The foundation is being laid, but the building is far from complete.

In 2025, that sense of purpose remained clear with disciplined progress and continued focus on long-term value creation. Those priorities remained central to the work of the Board throughout 2025 and continue to guide our stewardship of the institution.

Throughout 2025, the Group anniversary celebration brought together Ecobankers, partners, customers, governments, regulators and shareholders from across

Africa and beyond. We are deeply grateful to all who joined us in marking this occasion. Above all, we wish to recognise the staff across every corner of the continent whose dedication and professionalism make Ecobank what it is every single day. This anniversary belongs to every person who has built, served and sustained this institution at any time during the last four decades.

Chairman of the ETI Board with some of Ecobank's Founding Fathers during the Gala Dinner celebrating the 40th anniversary.

Our role as a Board is to provide oversight, to challenge management constructively, to ensure that risks are well understood, and to protect the interests of shareholders and the wider institution. It is equally to ensure that the Bank remains resilient, well-governed and strategically positioned to create sustainable long-term value. This remained an important responsibility in 2025.

The external environment in 2025 remained complex. African economies continued to face currency pressures, elevated financing costs and, in some markets, uneven growth.

Geopolitical uncertainties and the residual effects of prior years' fiscal stress in certain countries created ongoing headwinds for businesses and households across our footprint. Against this backdrop, the Group's performance was not only strong in absolute terms but particularly meaningful in context, reflecting the resilience of our diversified model, the depth of the pan-African franchise, and the quality of execution across the Group. Our pan-African model was built to not only remain operational and relevant but also to succeed precisely when conditions become difficult, and 2025 tested that premise, meaningfully.

In 2025, Ecobank delivered strong growth in key earnings measures. Net revenue increased by 17% to $2.45 billion and profit before tax rose by 21% to $801 million. Profit attributable to ETI shareholders increased by 22% to $407 million. Return on tangible equity was 27.8%, while the cost-to-income ratio improved on last year's record-setting pace to 48.3% in 2025. Earnings per share increased by 23% to 1.68 US cents ($0.017), while tangible book value per share rose by 82% to 7.65 US cents ($0.076).

For the Board, the main point is what these results say about the robustness of the Group and the progress being made in strengthening the institution. It is notable that 2025 is the third consecutive year in which Ecobank has delivered revenues above $2 billion, a threshold that was once aspirational and now reflects the durability of the Group's earnings platform. Beyond the headline numbers, the

Board's focus remains on the sustainability and quality of earnings, capital resilience and the consistency of value creation over time.

The market has taken notice: In 2025, ETI's share price soared by 49.6% in NGN terms (60.2% in dollar terms). And, over the last 24 months leading up to my letter in mid-May

NGN terms (323.9% in US dollar terms). This remarkable growth is a powerful testament to investors' confidence in the Group's fundamentals and bright future.

Our capital allocation approach remains clear: to preserve balance sheet strength, support priority investments and capital needs across the Group, and restore sustainable shareholder returns over time. Many of our more than

640,000 shareholders hold smaller positions and depend on dividends as an important part of their return, and the Board takes that responsibility seriously.

Capital and balance sheet resilience continued to receive close Board attention throughout the year. As at year-end, the Group's estimated CET1 ratio stood at 13.2% and total capital adequacy ratio at 16.8%, above regulatory minimum requirements. Customer deposits increased by 24% to $25.3 billion, further strengthening the Group's liquidity and funding profile. At the same time, the Board has continued to approach capital allocation with discipline and a long-term perspective, recognising that stronger performance does not by itself settle questions around capital flexibility, upstream capacity or distributable value. These matters still require prudence and careful judgement.

Nigeria remained an important area of Board focus during the year, as it has been for some time. The Board is encouraged by the progress made in strengthening the business and addressing legacy matters, in order to better seize the opportunity of Nigeria's long-term potential as

Africa's largest economy. Our approach continues to be disciplined and patient: we are focused on building a strong, well-governed franchise capable of generating consistent returns over time. Governance remained a priority in 2025. The Board continued to strengthen its oversight of risk governance, capital allocation, succession planning, executive accountability, remuneration alignment with performance, and regulatory engagement. Governance is not simply a matter of compliance for the Board; it is a strategic enabler of performance, confidence and institutional resilience.

The year also served as a reminder that strong institutions are tested as much in moments of scrutiny as in periods of growth. During 2025, the Board had to manage a challenging governance matter. Independent processes were activated promptly, proper procedures were followed in full, and the matter was handled in accordance with the highest standards of governance. The Board is satisfied that its response reflected the maturity that strong institutions must demonstrate when tested.

From our first subsidiary in Togo in 1988, we have grown into a pan-African banking group serving over 30 million customers across account-based and partnership channels, with total assets of $34.5 billion, customer deposits of $25.3 billion and gross loans of $12.8 billion.

Today, our footprint spans 34 sub-Saharan African countries and is complemented by operations in France, the United

Kingdom, United Arab Emirates and China, helping clients connect African opportunities to global trade and capital flows. Our network is one of our greatest strengths, not just because of its scale, but because of what it allows customers and clients to do: receive, save and move money, make payments, manage liquidity, finance trade and grow across borders.

What matters to us is not just growth, but the quality and durability of that growth. Our revenue base is broader and more balanced than before. In 2025, 42.4% of Group revenue came from non-interest revenue, while net fee and commission income accounted for 24.4% of net revenue. Net interest income increased to $1.4 billion, while non-interest revenue rose to $1.0 billion. Fee and commission income increased to $676 million. That tells us performance is being supported not only by lending, but also by payments, transaction services, treasury solutions and other fee-based activities across the franchise.

Customer deposits grew by 24%, reaching $25.3 billion, marking the fastest growth in over a decade. Most significantly, low-cost and stable current and savings accounts (CASA) now account for 87.1% of total customer deposits, thereby improving the quality of our funding base and lowering its cost. Gross loans increased by 22% to $12.8 billion, supported by trade finance, digitally enabled consumer lending and focused client activity in areas where we see attractive returns and strong demand. Total assets rose by 23% to $34.5 billion and total equity increased 27% to $2.9 billion. For shareholders, that supports a more durable earnings profile and a stronger platform for sustained returns over time.

Group equity attributable to ETI shareholders increased by $852 million to $1.93 billion. In the fourth quarter of 2025, as part of our measures to exit the Central Bank of Nigeria's regulatory forbearance, we took significant impairments in Nigeria, particularly within the CIB portfolio. This resulted in a pre-tax loss of $31 million for the year and a non-performing loan ratio of 42.1%. These actions made the turnaround more demanding in the near term, but they were necessary. They brought greater transparency to legacy issues and allowed us to address the difficult matters directly rather than carry them forward.

What matters now is execution. We are working closely with the regulator. We are actively managing capital and asset-quality issues and we are reshaping the business towards areas where we see better-quality growth. That shift is already visible. In 2025, CCB in Nigeria grew by 23% year on year, driven by deeper client engagement and growing transaction volumes. We still have work to do, but the path is clear: deal with the legacy issues, restore compliance, and build a better-quality business underneath.

Ecobank Foundation, we continued to support social impact across Africa, with programmes focused on health, education and gender equality.
